title: [DA] Rewrite the formula in the Strong SIV test
author: kasuga-fj
description:
In the Strong SIV test, given two addrecs `{c0,+,a}` and `{c1,+,a}`, the following inequality is evaluated:

`|c0 - c1| >s |a| * BTC`, where `BTC` is the backedge-taken count of the loop.

To evaluate this correctly, at least the following checks are necessary.

- `c0 - c1` doesn't overflow
- For all absolute-value calculations `|x|`, `x` is not the signed minimum value
- `|a| * BTC` doesn't overflow
- `0 <=s BTC`, which is currently missed
- The addrecs have `nsw`, which is also currently missed

Enumerating these conditions and inserting them one by one is risky, and I believe it makes the software flaky, so it should be avoided. It's also unclear if these conditions are sufficient.

This patch replaces the current implementation with one that uses `ConstantRange` so that we don't need to check any condition by ourselves.

Fixes the test case for the Strong SIV test added in #179664. I believe a similar fix can be applied for other tests as well.

tests.test_body:
define void @strong_siv_large_btc(ptr %A) {
entry:
  br label %loop.header

loop.header:                                      ; preds = %loop.latch, %entry
  %i.0 = phi i64 [ -9223372036854775808, %entry ], [ %i.0.inc, %loop.latch ]
  %i.1 = phi i64 [ -9223372036854775807, %entry ], [ %i.1.inc, %loop.latch ]
  %cond.0 = icmp eq i64 %i.0, 0
  br i1 %cond.0, label %if.then.0, label %loop.middle

if.then.0:                                        ; preds = %loop.header
  %gep.0 = getelementptr i8, ptr %A, i64 %i.0
  store i8 0, ptr %gep.0, align 1
  br label %loop.middle

loop.middle:                                      ; preds = %if.then.0, %loop.header
  %cond.1 = icmp eq i64 %i.1, 0
  br i1 %cond.1, label %if.then.1, label %loop.latch

if.then.1:                                        ; preds = %loop.middle
  %gep.1 = getelementptr i8, ptr %A, i64 %i.1
  store i8 0, ptr %gep.1, align 1
  br label %loop.latch

loop.latch:                                       ; preds = %if.then.1, %loop.middle
  %i.0.inc = add i64 %i.0, 1
  %i.1.inc = add i64 %i.1, 1
  %ec = icmp eq i64 %i.1.inc, 9223372036854775807
  br i1 %ec, label %exit, label %loop.header

exit:                                             ; preds = %loop.latch
  ret void
}

comments.body: @amehsan Could you please clarify whether you intend to block this PR? From my perspective, your objection seems to rely on your proof, which others have not agreed with so far. If you don't intend to block the PR, I would like to proceed with merging once another reviewer approves it, as I believe merging a PR with a single dissenting opinion is not uncommon.

Yes, please don't merge it for now. I think there are a couple of points that needs further discussions.

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
One major point of disagreement is what is the preconditions of the tests  (For now, let's focus on strongSIV):

A concern that you raised in a previous comment is this: https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/162281#discussion_r2537037457

> In principle, each dependence test assumes monotonicity over the entire iteration space..... I don't think this discrepancy immediately leads to unsoundness, I believe it's risky. I'm concerned that future changes could inadvertently introduce unsoundness due to this gap.

The point is that existing implementation doesn't have this assumption. But with your change here, you are adding this assumption to the test and your concern that I quoted above is valid _after the change you are proposing here._

@Meinersbur raised some concerns about my proof. Those concerns are mainly about how the proof is presented. I have re-written the proof and it is almost ready. Will post it later today.

The question that I have asked multiple times is still unanswered: How do you justify correctness of your change. Or for that matter how can one justify correctness of any of the DA tests? What tool do we have except a mathemtical proof to explain why a test is correct?

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
> One major point of disagreement is what is the preconditions of the tests (For now, let's focus on strongSIV):
> 
> A concern that you raised in a previous comment is this: [#162281 (comment)](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/162281#discussion_r2537037457)
> 
> > In principle, each dependence test assumes monotonicity over the entire iteration space..... I don't think this discrepancy immediately leads to unsoundness, I believe it's risky. I'm concerned that future changes could inadvertently introduce unsoundness due to this gap.
> 
> The point is that existing implementation doesn't have this assumption. But with your change here, you are adding this assumption to the test and your concern that I quoted above is valid _after the change you are proposing here._

I have been talking about cases with a condition such as `if (i < 1000)` the whole time. For the ZIV / SIV / RDIV tests, they are almost effectively assuming the monotonicity over the entire iteration space (from the first iteration to the last iteration). The assumption is not verified for now, but checking `nsw` will guarantee that. I don't see what would be the problem with this PR.

> How do you justify correctness of your change. Or for that matter how can one justify correctness of any of the DA tests? What tool do we have except a mathemtical proof to explain why a test is correct?

I explained the justification of the change in the PR description and in the comments in the code. There is a code review process in place to validate the correctness of changes. [The code-review policy states](https://llvm.org/docs/CodeReview.html#lgtm-how-a-patch-is-accepted) only approval from a single reiewer is required. As I understand it, it means that in almost all cases it is sufficient if two people (the author and the reviewer) agree that the change is correct.

If you think this is insufficient, then this concern is probably not specific to this PR. If you believe a formal proof is necessary for this change, I'd like to ask: why don't you request a formal proof for other PRs as well? I would also note that you do not provide a formal proof for your own PRs either.

If you are still not convinced, I recommend opening a thread on https://discourse.llvm.org/c/llvm, as already suggested in https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/176367#issuecomment-3814630183.

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
> I explained the justification of the change in the PR description and in the comments in the code. 

Yes, that is an outline of a proof. I am not saying having a proof is a must. I am saying it is a benefit. I don't understand the reasoning that tries to present a mathmatical proof as a weakness of an approach or as a  problem.

My argument here is that your patch relies on stronger assumptions for correctness of the test. That is a disadvantage. It may results in a future bug, or it may make future development more complicated.


> I have been talking about cases with a condition such as `if (i < 1000)` the whole time

from what I remember your loops has array indices like `i+j` and so they are not relevant to strong SIV discussion. If I have missed an example please let me know.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
To move forward: I strongly disagree with your approach, and will probably never support it. I feel like I've said this several times already, but the reasons are as follows:

- Your approach (and current implementation) has a lot of edge-cases. Adding validations for them one by one may make the code correct at the moment, but such an implementation is very fragile and can easily break in the future. This comes from my experience reviewing and developing DA. People may not read the comments or fully understand the edge-cases, so even if a condition like `SE->isKnownNonNegative(BTC)` is unintentionally removed, it would not be surprising at all.
- There is already agreement on fixing DA at the expense of its functionality (see https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-a-new-pass-as-an-alternative-to-dependenceanalysis/88403). In this context, a robust, reliable, easy-to-maintain, and simpler implementation is more important than extensibility. This is also the rationality behind delaying the introduction monotonicity. A complex algorithm like the one you are proposing should be discussed only after DA is fixed, stabilized (in other words, after it's enabled in the default pipeline).
- I don't expect the default behavior of SCEV to change, i.e., it will be guaranteed that nowrap flags are valid at every iteration. Thus your concern doesn't make much sense to me.

In addition, I feel that you still have not provided an adequate explanation of your concern. You haven't clarified the semantics of "nowrap flags of **addrec** under certain conditions". As I described in some previous comment, it would be very complex even harmful for DA. Moreover, even if such a feature were to be implemented, I don't think it would justify blocking this PR. As you also said, the feature is opt-in, and clients can choose which one to use. That is, it's possible using `const SCEV *` in this part while adopting `SCEVUse` in other parts of the StrongSIV test. I believe using `SCEVUse` in this part of the StrongSIV test would not be profitable.
Furthermore, even under your approach, using such context-sensitive SCEV expressions may be unsound -- these flags are not limited to addrecs. Given an addrec `{c,+,a}`, such context-sensitive flags can appear in `c` and `a` as well. This can affect the result of, for example,  `isKnownPredicate` queries. I'm not sure what it means, but in any case, whichever approach is taken, using such context-sensitive SCEV correctly would be very complex and far from trivial.

---

Let me ask again: are you still blocking this PR? If others agree with me, will you still block this PR?

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
FWIW, leaving my thoughts here. 

I think I am quite neutral on this one, I see the pros and cons of both approaches that are discussed here. If you would really press me for a preference, I would probably say Solution 2: existing code + additional BTC check, but probably for different reasons not mentioned before. The reason this would have my slight preference is that I find the equations easier to read and is almost exactly what you'd find in literature. This might be worth something, it might help with accessibility and maintainability of the code.  I understand the arguments that avoiding overflow check is a good thing so might be worth deviating from this, but on the other hand, the additional BTC check to existing code should be a straightforward change? That's why I am pretty much undecided, but with a small preference to stay closer to literature. 

The problem that I have is that I consider myself well informed in DA, but not the authority. I am uncomfortable to force a decision here. The problem is: who can?

Another argument that has been brought up: future extensions of SCEV. I find future directions a difficult argument, because making decisions based on things that may or may not happen at all is a bit tricky. But there's not a one size fits all, e.g. if there's a solid plan. For this SCEV extension https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/91961, I was going to say that this was proposed 2 years ago, so is unlikely to go anywhere. But as it happens @nikic has just expressed clear interest to pick this up soon. 

I haven't looked into the details why the SCEV extensions might be problematic for the approach proposed here, but if we focus on just that this change is now a lot more likely, can you explain what that means for this patch, @kasuga-fj ?

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
> FWIW, leaving my thoughts here.

Thanks for your comments. Two points:

1. I will try to clarify my reasoning based on the "future changes". If others find it not convincing, I am OK to take it back. Think about this situation: We need to allow per-use nsw flags 5 years down the road to catch some loop opt opportunity. We would need to rewrite the formula back to the existing form and also realize that this two forms are not equivalent. (Also consider that this situation likely exist for multiple other tests). _**While I agree that sacrificing precision for correctness is a good strategy I think when we have a provably correct solution that is also more precise, we should choose that.**_ Again, I am open to ignore this argument if others find it non-convincing.


1. The rewritten formula is not "Strong SIV" anymore as discussed in the literature. It is a different test close (but not equivalent) to one of the two parts of Strong SIV.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
> The reason this would have my slight preference is that I find the equations easier to read and is almost exactly what you'd find in literature. This might be worth something, it might help with accessibility and maintainability of the code. I understand the arguments that avoiding overflow check is a good thing so might be worth deviating from this, but on the other hand, the additional BTC check to existing code should be a straightforward change?

As for the accessibility, I think that's reasonable point, but I cannot fully agree with the rest. Yes, as you said, adding a BTC check could be a trivial change. However, what I want to emphasize here is that this is a completely separate issue from overflows. The condition `0 <=s BTC` is a really subtle one. I'm not trying to boast about it, but this case is something I happened to come up with one day, and it's really quite an obscure edge case. The fact that this check is necessary means that, with this approach, we need to take care of things other than overflows. I see this as a kind of technical debt. A small code change in the future could introduce other subtle corner cases. I'm still not sure if adding a BTC check is sufficient to cover all the edge cases in the current implementation in the first place. 

Roughly speaking, the motivation of this PR is: "let's delegate the handling of such edge cases to the SCEV framework, and just focus on the main (mathematical) logic in DA". Also, I believe there is value in deliberately choosing a form that differs from what appears in the literature. From the work we've done so far, it has become clear that there is a significant gap between the assumptions made in the literature and those in LLVM. I believe that intentionally not following the literature can, in a sense, help highlight that contextual difference between the two. Maybe I should add a comment in the code to explain this point.

> The problem that I have is that I consider myself well informed in DA, but not the authority. I am uncomfortable to force a decision here. The problem is: who can?

This is a very good point. Recently, the same question has come to my mind as well. So, this may sound presumptuous, but could you trust my judgment on this matter? I think it's fair to say that I've spent a decent amount of time investigating bugs in this area, and I'm a bit confident that I have a good understanding of the context and the issues involved. Given that background, I genuinely believe that the approach I'm proposing is the most reasonable one. (Perhaps it's time for us to formally decide on a maintainer of DA.)

> I haven't looked into the details why the SCEV extensions might be problematic for the approach proposed here, but if we focus on just that this change is now a lot more likely, can you explain what that means for this patch, @kasuga-fj ?

To be honest, I also still don't really understand why the SCEV extensions are considered a problem. The changes to SCEV mentioned here are not breaking changes, so they shouldn't cause any issues for DA. Perhaps the concern is about future extensions of DA, but I don't think we need to worry about that at this stage. Anyway, the SCEV extensions should be completely unrelated to this change (as well as to other changes in DA).

comments.author: kasuga-fj
amehsan
comments.body:
> As for the accessibility, I think that's reasonable point, but I cannot fully agree with the rest. Yes, as you said, adding a BTC check could be a trivial change. However, what I want to emphasize here is that this is a completely separate issue from overflows. The condition `0 <=s BTC` is a really subtle one. I'm not trying to boast about it, but this case is something I happened to come up with one day, and it's really quite an obscure edge case. The fact that this check is necessary means that, with this approach, we need to take care of things other than overflows. I see this as a kind of technical debt. A small code change in the future could introduce other subtle corner cases. I'm still not sure if adding a BTC check is sufficient to cover all the edge cases in the current implementation in the first place.

These are not corner cases discovered by trial and error. They come from investigation of the inequalities required. I was aware of the BTC condition before you mention it. If you look at the history of my proof, you can see that it says all the numbers should be interpreted as signed and it does say that non-nagtive values are less than BTC. I do give you credit for the example that you raised. Not because I didn't know BTC must be non-negative, but because I didn't know we need the check even with the existing SCEV behavior. But even then (and I believe I mentioned this to you before) my plan was to add test for BTC >= 0. Because I wanted to make sure the code will be correct when SCEV behavior changes.

That said, the point here is that, no, this test is not a technical debt and a small code change in the future is not going to introduce another subtle corner case. That is why we need a proof. Otherwise , this kind of cocern is applicable to any implemention including the one proposed here.

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
> The problem that I have is that I consider myself well informed in DA, but not the authority. I am uncomfortable to force a decision here. The problem is: who can?

I agree with this statement. Expertise in DA is not only a matter of reading  the code. There is more to it. Maybe it is not a discussion relevant to this PR, so I skip it, but I thought this is a good statment and I agree with it.

comments.author: amehsan
kasuga-fj
comments.body:
Sorry, I'm really not sure your argument about SCEV. Based on your past comments (including your [Discourse post](https://discourse.llvm.org/t/scev-question-about-inferring-nsw-flags/89858)[^foot]), I suspect there may be some fundamental misunderstanding about SCEV, though I’m not sure exactly what it is. The advantage you described for your approach simply doesn't make sense to me. I don't think further discussion will be productive, and I don't want to spend more time on this.

If you still believe your approach has an advantage with respect to future SCEV changes, please clarify the following points:

- What "the nowrap flags of **addrecs** under certain conditions" means.
- Why it is safe to employ context-sensitive nowrap flags in your approach.
- Under what circumstances your approach would be more beneficial than this one.

It's not directly related to this PR, but I'd also strongly recommend you to reread https://www.npopov.com/2023/10/03/LLVM-Scalar-evolution.html#nowrap-flags more carefully, at least to the extent that you can explain the points below:

- The difference between "inference" and "transformation" of the nowrap flags.
- What nowrap flags of addrecs actually mean.
    - In particular, which arithmetic operations are guaranteed not to overflow, and when.
- Why we cannot "transfer" the nowrap flags if the load/store isn't unconditionally executed.

---

> Not because I didn't know BTC must be non-negative, but because I didn't know we need the check even with the existing SCEV behavior.

This is really problematic. Most of the DA bugs we have found so far were due to misunderstandings of the behaviour of SCEV. "I didn’t know..." can be a serious issue.

> That said, the point here is that, no, this test is not a technical debt and a small code change in the future is not going to introduce another subtle corner case. That is why we need a proof. Otherwise , this kind of cocern is applicable to any implemention including the one proposed here.

As I’ve said repeatedly, the very existence of a proof is technical debt. It is extremely difficult to keep a proof up to date while preserving its correctness and consistency with the code. For example, how do we prevent a situation where the proof is accidentally modified and no longer proves the intended property? If you look at the codebase and/or its history, you can easily find several cases where the consistency between code comments and the actual code was broken.
There is no reason to adopt an approach that has so many edge cases that we cannot be confident in its correctness without a proof. We should strive to write code that is readable, maintainable, robust, and less likely to introduce defects (read [LLVM Code-Review Policy and Practices](https://llvm.org/docs/CodeReview.html)). Even the advantages of your approach, an adequate explanation has still not been provided.

[^foot]: This may be unsolicited advice, but I don't think a response is likely. The question is quite hard to follow, at least for me.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
@sjoerdmeijer (CC: @Meinersbur)

Sorry, could you please help? I'm fairly sure this discussion will not converge. I've asked @amehsan several times to provide an *appropriate* justification for the claimed advantages of their approach, but none has been provided so far. I don’t think we are likely to reach agreement, and I'm not convinced that further discussion would be particularly productive. Could you help mediate this?


Let me repeat myself: would you be willing to trust my judgment on this issue? I'm not trying to push my own idea; I simply believe this approach has the fewest corner cases and is the safest. As far as correctness *at this point* is concerned, both approaches are probably acceptable, but considering the quality of the implementation, I believe this approach is better. Also I don't think #91961 is related to this topic at all, and I don't understand why it was brought up.

Regarding enabling DA by default, if this approach is adopted, I would be reasonably confident in supporting it. With the other approach, however, I wouldn't be comfortable giving my approval (although I'm not trying to block it).

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
> Sorry, I'm really not sure your argument about SCEV.

I will reply to your comment by Monday.

comments.author: nikic
nikic
comments.body:
I'll also add that in the context of this test, I don't think that whether we have at-use nsw information or not is really going to be practically relevant. The test is only going to succeed if a and BTC are constant or have sufficiently small constant upper bounds -- aka exactly the situation where SCEV will be able to determine whether wrapping occurs based on simple range analysis that does not rely on IR flags. In my range-based formulation above, SCEV doesn't even bother checking the nowrap flags, it will explicitly check the wrapping behavior based on the step/BTC ranges.

For this test, I think you'll want to express it in the simplest way that works -- the precise way it is expressed is unlikely to affect practical analysis power.

As to whether DA can use at-use SCEV, I *think* it's possible in principle, but requires care. But as said before, I don't think it will make a meaningful difference in this context, so I don't think we need to get hung up about it.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
> Can we express the condition proposed here like this instead?
> 
> ```
> ConstantRange SrcRange = SE->getSignedRange(Src);
> ConstantRange DstRange = SE->getSignedRange(Dst);
> return SrcRange.intersectWith(DstRange).isEmptySet();
> ```
> 
> This leaves the heavy lifting to SCEV and does not require nowrap flags -- not that I expect it to be useful, but we _can_ prove disjointness of wrapping ranges as well. It's also not specific to _strong_ SIV in particular. This seems like something we should just be checking upfront to discharge the "obvious" cases that BasicAA does not catch itself.

Wow, great, I've not realized that API. Yes, we can use this representation here, and it seems simpler and better than I proposed. I will update the patch accordingly. Thanks for the suggestion! This representation would also be applicable to other SIV tests, but at the moment I'll just update the Strong SIV test because maybe we need to remove some of the existing features for the other tests (e.g., `weakCrossingSIVtest`, where some additional checks are performed to handle special cases).

> For this test, I think you'll want to express it in the simplest way that works -- the precise way it is expressed is unlikely to affect practical analysis power.

This statement sounds perfectly reasonable to me. This test can probably only catch trivial cases, and it's still unclear to me whether this test is useful in the real-world use cases in the first place. Thus I had even started to think that it might be better to remove it if we couldn’t reach consensus. But anyway, we should definitely use the simplest way to express it, even if there exists a way that is in theoretically more precise but more complicated and unlikely to be practically useful.

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
> I'll also add that in the context of this test, I don't think that whether we have at-use nsw information or not is really going to be practically relevant. The test is only going to succeed if a and BTC are constant or have sufficiently small constant upper bounds

This is correct. I also agree that this test is likely to have little impact in practice. However my argument was a little different:

step 1: _**the other test**_ in Strong SIV would benefit from at-use nsw information.
step 2: at-use nsw information will break _**this test**_ when written in this form. 

Conclusion: So to ensure DA is correct,  we will need to avoid at-use nsw information. This would prevent us from doing certain optimizations like the loop distribution example I gave before.

That said, if this is still not convincing, I am fine with this patch.

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
> step 1: the other test in Strong SIV would benefit from at-use nsw information.
> step 2: at-use nsw information will break this test when written in this form.

I believe that with the latest implementation in this PR, as it no longer blindly evaluates addrecs at BTC, using at-use information is fine now (assuming using it in DA is fine at all). So I think we don't need to be concerned that this change will negatively impact future improvements to DA.
